symbols: white dove descending — the Holy Spirit overshadowing Mary, the divine power effecting the Incarnation
white lilies in Gabriel's hand — Mary's perpetual virginity and purity, a traditional Annunciation attribute
open book on lectern — Mary interrupted in prayer or reading Scripture, her receptivity to God's Word
Gabriel kneeling — the angel's reverence before the chosen vessel of the Incarnation, the honor of the message
divine light radiating from above — the heavenly origin of the announcement, God breaking into human history
description: A dark interior with two main figures. On the left, a young woman in a blue robe over a red dress kneels at a wooden lectern with an open book, her left hand raised in a gesture of surprise or acceptance. On the right, a winged angel in white and blue-green robes kneels on a cloud, one arm raised toward heaven and the other extending white lilies toward the woman. Above them, a white dove descends in a burst of golden light that illuminates the otherwise shadowy room. A dark curtain hangs at the upper left. The scene is shown within an ornate gilded frame.
theological_description: The Annunciation is the pivotal moment of salvation history when the eternal Word becomes flesh (John 1:14). Gabriel's greeting 'Hail, full of grace' (Luke 1:28) initiates the New Covenant, and Mary's fiat — 'let it be done to me according to your word' — becomes the reversal of Eve's disobedience, a patristic theme developed by Irenaeus. The dove of the Holy Spirit descending recalls the Spirit hovering over the waters in Genesis 1:2, marking this as a new creation. The lilies signify Mary's virginal purity, fulfilling Isaiah 7:14. The open book points to Mary as the one steeped in Scripture, the Daughter of Zion who embodies Israel's hopes. This moment is simultaneously the humiliation of God (kenosis, Philippians 2:7) and the exaltation of humanity — God taking human nature through a woman's consent.

scripture_refs.reason: The angel Gabriel sent to Mary in Nazareth announcing the conception of Jesus
The virgin shall conceive and bear a son — the prophecy fulfilled in the Annunciation
The Word became flesh and dwelt among us — the Incarnation inaugurated here
Christ emptying himself, taking the form of a servant — the kenosis beginning at conception
